California
Fair Political
Practices Comrnission
December 19, 199 0
Darryl R. wold
Attorney at Law
3151 Airway Avenue, suite M-l-
Costa Mesa, CA 92626
Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
our File No. I-9 0-58 6
Dear Mr. wold:
You have requested advice on behal,f of Brad Gates, Sheriff of
orange county, regarding the use of campaiqn funds under the
Politica1 Reform Act (the trAct").r/ 
Specifically, you have asked
the commission to reconsider its earlier telephone advice to your
ctient and aLter its interpretation of Section 85802(f). Since
your question is general in nature, vre are treating your request
as one for informal assistance pursuant to Regulation 18329 (c)
(copy enclosedl .2/ This letter confirms the oral advice we
provided you on Friday, November 2, L99o.
OUESTION
Is it perrnissible for Sheriff Gates to use campaign funds to
pay for the cost of an annual christmas luncheon for his agency's
top employees and for the lunch of the enployee's spouse or guest?
The cost of each lunch e/ouLd be less than $50, and the cost of the
lunch for the employee and spouse or guest, together, would be
less than $100.
L/ covernment Code Sections 81OOO-91015. Al-l statutory
references are to the covernnent Code unless otherwise indicated.
Conrnj.ssion regulations appear at Z California Code of Regulations
Section 18OOO, et seq. AIl references to regulations are to fitle
2, Division 6 of the California code of Regulations.
2/ fnformal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
inmunity provided by an opinion or forrnal writtJn advice.
(Section 83113,. Regulation 18329(c) (3).)
428 J street, suite 8oo . p.o- Box 8oz . sacramento cA 958o4-o8 o? . (916) 322-5660


Our Fi■e No. I-90-586
Page 2
CONCLUSTON
Sheriff cates may use campaign funds to pay the cost of an
annual christmas luncheon for his agency's employees. Horrever,
Sheriff Gates may not use canpaign funds to pay the cost of the
lunch for either the enployee's spouse or for any other guest of
the enployee unless he can demonstrate, on some basis other than
the relationship to the agency employee, that the gift to such
spouse or guest is directly related to a political, 
legislative,
or governmental purPose.
FACTS
on July 23, :-990, you called this office requesting advj-ce on
behalf of Sheriff Gates. At that tirne you told staff that
Sheriff cates \"/anted to know whether it was permissible for him to
use his campaign funds to pay the cost of an annual christmas
luncheon for his top agency employees and for their spouses. In
your tetter of September 6, 1990, you presented an additional item
for our consideration - whether carnpaiqn funds may be used to pay
the cost of the luncheon for guests of these employees.
You accurately surnmarized the tel-ephone advice given to you
on Jul.y 24 , L99O. Carnpaign funds nay be used to pay for the cost
of the Christrnas luncheon for his top agency ernployees under
Section 85202(f) (2). 
Hos/ever, campaign funds nay not be used to
pay for the cost of the lunch for the invited guests of these
enployees. This prohibition incLuded spouses. Such an
expenditure of campaign funds would not be construed as being
directly related to a potitical, 
legislative, or governmental
purpose under Section 85202 (f) (1).
In your letter, you request that we reconsider our telephone
advj-ce. You urge another interpretation of Section 85802(f) which
you state as follows:
Pernitting an ernployee to invite a spouse or
guest to attend an event $rith that employee, at the
empl-oyee's option, should be considered a gift to
the enployee, and therefore be ,directly related,
to a perrnJ,tted purpose under the specific
provj,sions of S 85802(f) (2) pernitting gifts to
employees of an elected officer,s agency . . .
Your position is based on the premise that the tota] cost for the
employee and his or her guest would be less than the g100
linitation. 
You contend that this type of a gift, the extension
of the invitation to the spouse or other designated guest, is
being done sole1y for the benefit of the enployee, accoraingly,
the Christnas luncheon should be construed as one gift to the
enployee, rather than as two separate gifts to tr{o individuals.


Our Fi■e No. ■-90-586
Page 3
ANALYSI S
The issue presented for our consideration is not $rhether a
public official, may make gifts to his employees and.to their
-spouses or guests, but rather, whether a pubtic officiaL may use
campaiqn funds for that PurPose.
Effective January 1, 1990, the Act was amended by senate Bill
t-431 to j,nclude new provisions that regulate the appropriate use
of campaign funds. (Section 85800, et seq. ) The purPose of the
reqislition was itto ensure that candidates, elected officers and
the people clearl,y recognize acceptable and unacceptable uses of
carnpaign funds.tr (Senate Elections committee Analysis of SB
r-431. )
The generaL rule of the new personal use law is that any
expendituie of campaign funds nust be, at a minirnum, reasonably
related to a political-, tegislative, or governmental purpose'
(Section 85801.) However, where an expenditure confers a
substantiat personal benefit on the candidate or officeholder, the
expenditure inust be directly related to a political, 
Iegislative,
or governmentaL purpose. (Section 85801.) A substantial personal
uen6rit is definld ls a direct Personal benefit to the candidate
or officeholder of $1oo or more' (Seetion 85800(b) (2).)
Moreover, the new personal use law sets forth provisions
dealing with specific types of expenditures. Section 85802(f),
which deals with gifts, provides in pertinent part:
(1) Canrpaign funds sha1l not be used to make
personal gifts unless the gift is directly related
to a political, 
legislative, or governmental
purpose.
(2) An electj.on victory celebration or
sirnilar campaign event, or gifts totaling less than
one hundred dollars (S100) in a calendar year nade
to an enployee or a canpaign committee worker, or
to an employee of the elected off.icer's aqtency, are
considered to be directly related to a political,
J.egislative, or lrovernmental purpose.
Thus, the general rule governing the use of campaign funds to
make a gift is set forth in section 85202(f) (1). It provi.des for
a direct relationship between the pernissible expenditure of
carnpai.gn funds and the purpose. Section 85202(f) (2) expressLy
designates individuals excluded fron the strict rule contaj-ned in
Section 85202(f) (1). It provides, in part, that a gift totaling
less than S1OO to an enpLovee of the elected officerrs agency is
deerned to be directly related to a political, 
legislative, 
or
governnenta). purpose. Section 85802(f) (2) is silent concerni.ng
the spouse of an eruployee. Presurnably then, using carnpaign funds
to rnake personal glfts to any individual other than the enployee


Our Fi■e No. I-90-586
Page 4
requires the dj-rect relationship to a permitted purpose test set
forth in section 85802(f) (1).
Your analysis urges the conmission to create an exception to
the general rul,e set forth in section 85802(f) (1) and adopt an
interpretation of Section 85802(f) (2) that goes beyond the express
language and intent of the Legislature. Exceptions to the general
rule must be narrowly construed. (see Estate of Baneriee (1978)
21 Cal,. 3d 527, 540.)
The most recent legislative action concerning restrictions 
on
the personaL use of canpaign funds has clarified further
legislative intent concerning the scope of individuals covered by
se6tj-on 85802(f) (2).3 I Effective January l, L9gL, Section
85802(f) ( renumbered 89513(f)) wiJ.1 read as follows:
(1) Campaign funds shall not be used to make
personal gifts unless the gift is directly related
to a political, 
legislative, or governnental
purpose .
(2) An election victory celebration or
sinilar carnpaign event, or gifts with a total
cumulative value of less than two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) in a single year made to an
individual- enployee, a campai,gn cornmittee worker,
or an employee of the elected officer's agency, are
considered to be directly related to a political,
legislative, or governmental purpose. For purposes
of this paragraph, a gift to a member of a person's
immediate farnily shall be deemed to be a gift to
that purpose.
( Emphasis added. )
For purposes of our analysis, the important change to be
noted is that members of an individual's immediate family will be
covered. Section 82o29 provides that immediate fanily rneans the
spouse and dependent chj.Idren. This change is consistent with the
desiqnated individuals covered in other t'personal- userr
provisions.l/ 
rf the Legislature had intended to extend coverage
of Section 85802(f) to an imrnediate fanily member prior to
January 1, L991, it would have done so.
3/ The ethics bill 
anended, in part, the political 
Reform Act.
The new law moves the rrpersonal userr provisions from Chapter 5 to
a new Chapter 9.5.
4/ See, Section 85802(a) - travel. expenses; Section 85802(b) (2)
health-related evpenses; Section 85802(e) - cost of purchase of
tickets to political fundraising events,. and Section 85802(e) (1)
cost of tickets for entertainrnent or sporting events.


our File No. I -9 O-58 6
Page 5
Under the current Ialr, an expenditure of campaign funds to
make personal gifts totaling less than $1oo in a calendar year to
an employee of the elected officer's aqency is deened to be
directly related to a political, 
legislative, or governmental
purpose. (Section 85802(f) (2).) 
Therefore, it is pernissible for
sheriff Gates to use campaigtn funds under the current law to pay
the cost of an annual christmas luncheon for his agency's top
empl,oyees. However, it is not pernissible for hin to use canrpaign
funds under the current Iaw to pay the cost of the lunch for the
employee's spouse or for any other invited guest of the employee,
unless he can denonstrate, on some basis other than the
relationship to the employee, that the gift to such spouse or
guest is diiectly related to a potitical , legislative, 
or
governmental purPose.
If you have any further guestions regarding this matter,
please do not hesitate to call ne at (916) 322-590]-.
S incere 1y,
Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
,Ac,*r-*y,/'fn*-
By: Deanne stone
counsel, Legal Division
SH: DS : aA
Enc losure
